issue_title: Slow task signature check and large task `~/.sos` folder
issue_body:
Also reported by @jean997 for a cluster specific feature. After doing lots of simulations on the cluster the external task signature managed by `sos task` accumulates to the point that it appears to be hanging when submitting new tasks. 

As discussed on slack, ways to resolve the issue is:

1. Automatically clean up old tasks (but how often? after every run is overkill)
2. Use time stamps instead just like snakemake (but will have problems of skewed time stamp -- time stamps is not reliable across machines)

So compromised alternatives are:

1. Clean up tasks at intervals of days.
2. Use time stamps for failed and completed tasks only. 

I think this should solve most of the problem though there are still room to optimize. Will apply these changes later and see what happens. If it is still to conservative and performance still is an issue, we can certainly explore more aggressive options.

https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/8e960ca203bd5d8c48e129c4172dd2b13cdc4439/store/gateway/bin/boot#L128-L132
https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/8e960ca203bd5d8c48e129c4172dd2b13cdc4439/registry/bin/boot#L89-L93
Both these components have the same problem: lock may expire between being checked and being updated. I see following in my logs:

issue_title: Application healthcheck endpoint for checks during deploy and publishing
issue_body:
During an application release/deploy, the container just checks to make sure that the fleet unit reports that it is running: https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/c2cffb969a1edaaa456033a05b7a6958eea02bad/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L193

Just because a unit is running doesn't mean that it's ready/healthy.

Additionally, there is actually a bug in the way that publisher checks for applications to be listening before it publishes them - @gabrtv discovered that the Docker proxy responsible for routing requests from the host into containers will actually accept on the external port immediately (even if no daemon in the container is bound to that port). 

The proposal is to:
- Add a new `healthcheck` endpoint and CLI command to configure a healthcheck endpoint for the application
- Modify the controller to wait for each deployed container to report healthy if a healthcheck endpoint is configured for the application
- Modify the publisher to check for an appropriate (HTTP 200?) response from the healthcheck endpoint before publishing an app container if a healthcheck endpoint is configured for the application

This solution addresses several issues and feature requests surrounding application deploys and health checks in the router, including #3478, #3723, and #3674.

issue_title: Attempt to move off of `six`
issue_body:
Because `six==1.4.1` is embedded in the OSX system python, we get a conflict with our included version if someone does a `sudo pip install globus-sdk` on OSX. This is also described in #45
The result is that our six is installed behind the system version, and isn't the one loaded at import time.

Solutions that have been batted around include just doing doc about virtualenv ( #45 ), trying to modify `sys.path`, and moving off of `six` altogether.
Although the virtualenv is a best practice, and ultimately the safest solution, it's desirable to let people do the global pip install if we can support it without too much effort.
The push to do that becomes somewhat stronger when you consider that these dependencies are inherited by the CLI.

The current usage sites for `six` are as follows:

- `six.add_metaclass` on base Authorizer
- `six.moves.configparser` in config loading
- `six.moves.urllib.parse` in a couple of locations
- `six.Iterator` as a parent of `PaginatedResource`

The use of `six.moves` is pretty easy to work around, since the things that are renamed generally haven't changed their signatures. Even if they have, we can wrap methods with our own helpers to handle this.
[six.Iterator](https://pythonhosted.org/six/#six.Iterator) is a convenience like the `six.moves` module, so that's also not a big deal.

If we had meaningful things in `six.add_metaclass`, this could get really painful trying to define 2-to-3 compatible metaclasses, but we're just using it to make an abstract base class non-instantiable.
That's not really important -- having all of its methods raise `NotImplementedError` communicates the same thing.

Unless there's a gotcha somewhere in this, we should just go ahead and do it.
This does *not* obsolete #45 -- that's still a best practice and we should encourage it.

When using the `spawn` method (our default) for the multiprocessing module, I often get a bunch of unhandled KeyboardInterrupt exceptions when I Ctrl+C trinity. If using `TRINITY_MP_CONTEXT=fork` I never seem to get those

```
    INFO  06-04 15:06:13       chain  Imported 192 headers in 2.25 seconds, new head: #1346212 (1cd32f)                                                                         
^C    INFO  06-04 15:06:14        main  Keyboard Interrupt: Stopping                                                                                                            
Process Process-2:3:                                                                                                                                                            
Process Process-2:1:                                                                                                                                                            
    INFO  06-04 15:06:14         ipc  Sent SIGINT to process 27860, waiting 5 seconds for it to terminate                                                                       
Process Process-2:2:                                                                                                                                                            
Traceback (most recent call last):                                                                                                                                              
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 258, in _bootstrap                                                                                                 
    self.run()                                                                                                                                                                  
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 93, in run                                                                                                         
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)                                                                                                                                   
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/concurrent/futures/process.py", line 169, in _process_worker                                                                                         
    call_item = call_queue.get(block=True)                                                                                                                                      
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/queues.py", line 93, in get                                                                                                          
    with self._rlock:                                                                                                                                                           
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/synchronize.py", line 96, in __enter__                                                                                               
    return self._semlock.__enter__()                                                                                                                                            
KeyboardInterrupt                                                                                                                                                               
Traceback (most recent call last):                                                                                                                                              
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 258, in _bootstrap                                                                                                 
    self.run()                                                                                                                                                                  
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 93, in run                                                                                                         
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)                                                                                                                                   
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/concurrent/futures/process.py", line 169, in _process_worker                                                                                         
    call_item = call_queue.get(block=True)                                                                                                                                      
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/queues.py", line 94, in get                                                                                                          
    res = self._recv_bytes()                                                                                                                                                    
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/connection.py", line 216, in recv_bytes                                                                                              
    buf = self._recv_bytes(maxlength)                                                                                                                                           
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/connection.py", line 407, in _recv_bytes                                                                                             
    buf = self._recv(4)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/connection.py", line 379, in _recv
    chunk = read(handle, remaining)
KeyboardInterrupt
```

it looks like the kind of thing that would be caused by the [multiprocessing module](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/multiprocessing.html#multiprocessing.Semaphore):

> If the SIGINT signal generated by Ctrl-C arrives while the main thread is blocked by a call to BoundedSemaphore.acquire(),
Lock.acquire(), RLock.acquire(), Semaphore.acquire(), Condition.acquire() or Condition.wait() then the call will be immediately
interrupted and KeyboardInterrupt will be raised.

issue_title: doc/tutorials/demo.rst typo
issue_body:
[demo.rst](https://github.com/epfl-lts2/pygsp/blob/master/doc/tutorials/demo.rst) says:

> You can now access the eigenvalues of the fourier basis with G.e and the eigenvectors G.U, they look like sinuses on the graph. Let's plot the second and third eigenvector, as the one is only constant.

But then, since the index starts at 0, it plots the third and the fourth ones:

```
pygsp.plotting.plt_plot_signal(G, G.U[:, 2], savefig=True, vertex_size=50, plot_name='doc/tutorials/img/logo_second_eigenvector')
pygsp.plotting.plt_plot_signal(G, G.U[:, 3], savefig=True, vertex_size=50, plot_name='doc/tutorials/img/logo_third_eigenvector')
```

issue_title: Activate Saved Environments on Windows
issue_body:
Activating enviroments on Windows execute the batch scripts in ```..\etc\conda\activate.d```, but the variables can't be used in the environment.

Example of terminal;

```
C:\Users\admin\Documents\pipeline>activate test
Deactivating environment "C:\Users\admin\Documents\pipeline\miniconda"...
Activating environment "C:\Users\admin\Documents\pipeline\miniconda\envs\test"..
.
C:\Users\admin\Documents\pipeline\miniconda\envs\test\etc\conda\activate.d\

[test] C:\Users\admin\Documents\pipeline>echo %SOMETHING%
%SOMETHING%

[test] C:\Users\admin\Documents\pipeline>
```

with this ```env_vars.bat``` in ```..\etc\conda\activate.d```;
```
set SOMETHING=%~dp0
echo %SOMETHING%
```

issue_title: numpydoc=0.5 contains embedded sphinx=1.2.3
issue_body:
When creating an environment like this:

`conda create -n testenv python=2.7 sphinx numpydoc`

Conda will install sphinx 1.3.1 and numpydoc 0.5 just fine.

However, the environment will have both sphinx 1.2.3 and sphinx 1.3.1 in the site-packages, and if you import sphinx you'll get 1.2.3.  Apparently the 1.2.3 version is embedded within numpydoc!

Here's a fixed version: https://anaconda.org/prkrekel/numpydoc

issue_title: rpy2 package is broken on Py3.5, bug has been fixed upstream.
issue_body:
When loading the IPython magic from rpy2 on python 3.5, the current result is (trimming the traceback):

```
/Users/fperez/usr/conda/lib/python3.5/site-packages/rpy2/robjects/__init__.py in <module>()
     17 
     18 from rpy2.robjects.robject import RObjectMixin, RObject
---> 19 from rpy2.robjects.vectors import (BoolVector,
     20                                    IntVector,
     21                                    FloatVector,

/Users/fperez/usr/conda/lib/python3.5/site-packages/rpy2/robjects/vectors.py in <module>()
    334 
    335 
--> 336 class StrVector(Vector, StrSexpVector):
    337     """      Vector of string elements
    338 

TypeError: type 'rpy2.rinterface.StrSexpVector' is not an acceptable base type
```

This particular bug is [known and already fixed in rpy2 upstream, since version 2.7.4](https://bitbucket.org/rpy2/rpy2/issues/312).  The current version of rpy2 as of Apr 18, 2016 is [2.7.8](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/rpy2). Unfortunately conda still ships the outdated 2.7.0.

issue_title: [FR] Conda should have more aggressive HTTP caching
issue_body:
It would be nice to have more aggressive HTTP caching in conda (and repo.continuum.io and conda.anaconda.org), especially for the `fetch_index` call that occurs at the beginning of many user interactions. With a couple channels added, this often takes ~1000ms for me.

1. Currently, my understanding is that some caching is used only when the user supplies a special flag (`--use-index-cache`), which I assume is rare. But even this cache is pretty ineffective.
2. On anaconda.org, the `repodata.json.bz2` file is not served with any ETag or Expires headers.
3. On `https://repo.continuum.io/`, the `repodata.json.bz2` file is served with ETag, but not Expires headers. Without the Expires headers, you can't avoid a trip to the server, even if the response is cached.
4. Instead of manually operating a cache, as is done in `fetch_repodata()`, all of this could be done transparently with a library like [CacheControl](http://cachecontrol.readthedocs.org/en/latest/index.html). IIRC, this is what pip does as well. But obviously it requires the right HTTP headers, especially Expires.

issue_title: Make it possible (and intuitive) to install a package directly from search results
issue_body:
After performing a search like `anaconda search -t conda python-geohash`, and getting some results, it's not obvious to newbies what needs to be done next in order install the package displayed in the search results and searching this way is the default route that newbies take when a package is not found since it is what is suggested in the terminal when an installation command fails to come up with a package matching the name typed by the user.

I would maybe recommend putting numbers next to the search results and offering users to press that number for it to start installing.

issue_title: conda clone fails when repo url changes
issue_body:
Conda has new strict channel urls for each installed package but this functionality breaks when running your own Anaconda Repo and then migrating to a new hostname/server.  All of the old packages have explicit urls to a hostname that no longer exists and it fails to perform the clone operation even though all of those packages exist in the new server at its new hostname.

We need to handle this better and if we cannot connect to the old url, then check again using the channels configured in `.condarc` for the same package.  Currently it just fails and will not allow you to clone old environments.

```
conda create --name newenv --clone oldenv
Source: /user/anaconda/envs/oldenv
Destination: /user/anaconda/envs/newenv
Packages: 175
Files: 2278
Fetching package metadata ...Could not connect to http://oldhostname:port/pkgs/linux-64/
.Could not connect to http://oldhostname:port/pkgs/noarch/
.Could not connect to http://oldhostname:port/conda/t/$TOKEN/channame/linux-64/
.Could not connect to http://oldhostname:port/conda/t/$TOKEN/channame/noarch/

```

issue_title: Support monitor mode
issue_body:
Hello,

We are [studying a cleanup in scapy's code](https://github.com/secdev/scapy/pull/1350), and were comparing currently "up-to-date" python libpcap implementations, to see which were outdated. (Indeed we are currently supporting python-pypcap, python-libpcap, pcapy, dnet and dumbnet)

Pcapy is one of the most up-to-date and maintained alternatives we have, and really is a great tool. However, unlike `python-libpcap` or `python-pypcap` ([PR](https://github.com/pynetwork/pypcap/pull/67)), pcapy has no support for monitor mode (`pcap_set_rfmon`)

As said by @guyharris in https://github.com/CoreSecurity/pcapy/issues/19#issuecomment-301648583, it would require a deeper implementation, that is currently really lacking to pcapy. This has also been reported by https://github.com/CoreSecurity/pcapy/issues/17.

I know that many people have other things to do, but it would really be great if CoreSecurity/pcapy had plans about supporting all those functions.

Thank you for reading, really hoping that this project will move on, and not die like all the others.

issue_title: Lomond logging
issue_body:
My package which uses lomond utilizes python logging. If I set my internal logger's log level to debug I also get lomond debug output. This is how I configure my logger.

```
def configure_logger(loggerid=None, debug=False):
	if loggers.get(loggerid):
		return loggers.get(loggerid)
	else:
		level = logging.DEBUG if debug == True else logging.INFO

		logger = logging.getLogger(loggerid)
		handler = logging.StreamHandler()
		formatter = logging.Formatter("%(levelname)s %(message)s")
		handler.setFormatter(formatter)
		logger.addHandler(handler)
		logger.setLevel(level)
		logger.propagate = False
		loggers[loggerid] = logger
	return logger
```
This SHOULD isolate my logger from lomond's as I am setting propagate to false. Am I missing something here?

issue_title: Missing header for SAM output?
issue_body:
Not sure if this is intentional or not, but looks like when writing SAM (but not BAM) files the header is omitted. For instance, following the example from readthedocs: 

```
inbam = pysam.AlignmentFile("test/test_data/small.lmna.bam", "rb")
pairedreads = pysam.AlignmentFile("allpaired.bam", "wb", template=inbam)
for read in inbam.fetch():
     if read.is_paired:
             pairedreads.write(read)

pairedreads.close()
inbam.close()
```

...this works as expected and creates a correctly formatted BAM file.  However, replacing the second line with
```
pairedreads = pysam.AlignmentFile("allpaired.sam", "w", template=inbam)
```
results in a file with no header and just the reads. 

Seems a bit inconsistent, but perhaps there's a good reason for it?

issue_title: Regression: Exception when creating VUnit instance when no simulator is installed
issue_body:
Checking for the simulator being installed should be delayed until the simulator is actually needed. I am guessing that there is some coupling between detection of what simulator is available and the UI/arguments that are supported relating to that simulator.

For example, if you simply wanted to create a VUnit project: `prj = VUnit.from_argv()` -- why do you need to have a supported simulator installed?

I believe there is a regression introduced in cc534bdf752a9cd5dec0e42e52b97abe3e0ae531. This issue was originally fixed in 4bbd629b1ac86038c47715e0688a27c17631cc63, related to #67 

Similarly -- It might be nice to also have a command line argument which can ignore that a supported simulator has not been detected.

issue_title: '+' character in document index replaced with space character in low-level client index operation
issue_body:
Indexing a document into elasticsearch using the low-level client as such:
```
es_client = elasticsearch.Elasticsearch([ES_URL])
es_client.index(
    index=index, doc_type=doc_type,
    id='2017-04-14T16:04:45.000000+0000',
    body=body
)
```
results in an indexed document where `_id=2017-04-14T16:04:45.000000 0000`. The `+` character is being replaced with a space.

Using the bulk helper performs the indexing correctly:
```
from elasticsearch.helpers import bulk
action = [{
    '_id': '2017-04-14T16:04:45.000000+0000',
    '_index': index,
    '_type': doc_type,
    '_source': body       
}]
bulk(es_client, action)
```

Version: `elasticsearch==5.3.0`

issue_title: [ACR] 'az acr build-task run' missing --no-wait
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**

Executing the `az acr build-task run` command with `--no-logs` blocks, returning only when the build has completed. Without a `--no-wait` arg for this command, automation scripts are blocked until the build completes. In some cases this is desirable (such as for dependencies further down in the script requiring the built images), but in others, blocking is an unnecessary and unwanted delay.

**To Reproduce**

1. Create a build-task
   ```sh
   az acr build-task create \
     --registry $ACR_NAME \
     --name buildhelloworld \
     --image helloworld:{{.Build.ID}} \
     --context https://github.com/$GIT_USER/acr-build-helloworld-node \
     --branch master \
     --git-access-token $GIT_PAT
   ```
1. Run the build-task
  `az acr build-task run --registry $ACR_NAME --name buildhelloworld --no-logs`
1. Witness "Running," and the command blocks until build has succeeded.
1. Run the build task, but add `--no-wait` to attempt to suppress blocking behavior
  Result: **az: error: unrecognized arguments: --no-wait**

**Expected behavior**

The `--no-wait` arg should disable the blocking behavior of the command.

**Environment summary**

* Install Method: *.deb
* CLI version: 2.0.43
* OS version: Ubuntu 18.04
* Shell Type: Bash

**Additional context**

This command did not block originally, but its behavior was changed in #6578.

issue_title: Looking for a better way to do things on behalf of another subscription
issue_body:
If I have multiple Azure accounts and multiple subscriptions, and I'm trying to manage all of these with the same backend application using this SDK, I need to go into each subscription and perform the following steps to retrieve the highlighted information:
* Go to the Azure Portal and create a new App Registration.
* When that is complete look at the details of the application and get the **application id**.
* Go to the *Keys* section, generate a new **secret key** and store that number.
* Go to the *Endpoints* section - this is very not intuitive btw - and examine the URLs that come up to retrieve the part of the URL that acts as my **tenant id**.
* Look up my subscription details and pull the **subscription id**.

Once all of this is complete, execute an Azure CLI command or PowerShell script that will then grant permissions needed for the new application to do its job.

These are A LOT of numbers to store and keep track of for each subscription. I'd like to see something closer to what Amazon AWS does with IAM Roles, where each account that wants to grant you permissions creates an IAM *Role* and gives the account that's doing the work a global identifier for that role. That process that does the work then authenticates to Amazon with its own set of secret keys, and performs an *assumeRole* action to take on the credentials granted by the identifier for that role.

Beyond just permissions, the identifier itself represents a link between two accounts, and the assumeRole action also takes an "external id" field which can be used to further validate the account that's trying to assume those permissions.

This would greatly reduce the complexity of monitoring and management software that we may write for our organizations.

issue_title: Support list of regexps for `exclude` configuration
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm running into a use case where we have a regex disabling the use of the ruby HTTParty library across (because we built a wrapper around it that handles our proxy settings). However, in some rare cases, we want to specifically use it (for instance because we're posting to localhost, or something in the internal network), and the exclude option for this hook has been growing and is not very elegant:
```yaml
- id: no_httparty
  exclude: ^(path_to_some_file_which_has_a_good_reason_to_use_httparty.rb|some_other_file.rb|...|...|...)$
```

It would be nice it there would be support for either:
- specifying `exclude` a list of patterns:
```yaml
- id: no_httparty
  exclude:
    - ^path_to_some_file_which_has_a_good_reason_to_use_httparty.rb$
    - ^some_other_file.rb$
    - ...
```
- supporting inline disable for pre-commit hooks (maybe in the way pep8 or rubocop do it)

Let me know if one of those options seems reasonable, I'd be happy to give a shot at a PR.

Thanks

issue_title: Deprecate and remove some (useless?) options
issue_body:
I find the following don't really have any good use cases (and don't come up in normal day-to-day) and are undocumented beyond `--help`.  **I'm proposing removing these options**:

## `pre-commit run --no-stash`

This disables the auto-stashing of files when running -- though this is already the case for `pre-commit run --all-files` and `pre-commit run --files ...`.

The behaviour of `--no-stash` (without using `--no-stash`) can be achieved via `git diff --name-only | xargs pre-commit run --files`

It was added [along with the avoiding behaviour](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/pull/80) in the same pull request.  I want to say this was my first idea for "fixing" the [original problem](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/68) and then I forgot to undo it.

## `pre-commit run --allow-unstaged-config`

This (unfortunately) collides with `pre-commit run --all` (prefix match of `pre-commit run --all-files`) so I've wanted to get rid of it anyway.

This allows one to run with an unstaged configuration, which then (in most cases) gets the changes swiped out from under you and causes confusing situations where the hooks that are run aren't what was on disk at the time of running.  The warning that's printed when doing this also explains this.

This was [originally my idea](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/157#issuecomment-99080756) but now I think we can just do without the option at all -- requiring the pre-commit configuration to be staged when running pre-commit.

issue_title: GunicornWebWorker: worker_abort not called on WORKER TIMEOUT event
issue_body:
## Long story short

In #1420, code was added to call the `worker_abort` callback when receiving the `SIGABRT` signal.

It works correctly, except when the worker is *blocked by a sync call*, which is typically the case on `WORKER TIMEOUT` event.

Thus, it is hard to catch such timeouts and send an error report.

## Expected behaviour

* In its loop running every second, Gunicorn's `Arbiter.murder_workers` detects a worker that was silent for too long (`timeout` setting)
* the arbiter calls `self.kill_worker(pid, signal.SIGABRT)` which should be received by `GunicornWebWorker.handle_abort`
* the `worker_abort` callback defined in the gunicorn config file should be called

## Actual behaviour

* the arbiter calls `self.kill_worker(pid, signal.SIGABRT)` on a blocked worker, nothing happens immediately
* the arbiter runs its loop 1 second later, and calls `self.kill_worker(pid, signal.SIGKILL)`
* the `GunicornWebWorker` worker is killed without calling any callbacks

## Idea to fix

Around the following code:
 https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/74810c2f9fdd459fadde0ae3d43c06dcae58064f/aiohttp/worker.py#L136-L137

Using `signal.signal(signal.SIGABRT, self.handle_abort)` instead of `self.loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGABRT, self.handle_abort, signal.SIGABRT, None)` may help, but I don't know how important it is to keep the signal handling attached to the event loop.

## Steps to reproduce

server.py:
```python
from aiohttp import web
import time

async def index(request):
    time.sleep(5)
    return web.Response(text='Hello Aiohttp!')

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/', index)
```

gunicorn_config.py:
```python
worker_class = "aiohttp.GunicornWebWorker"
accesslog = "-"
errorlog = "-"

def worker_abort(worker):
    worker.log.info("CONFIG: worker received SIGABRT signal")
```

* run `gunicorn server:app --timeout 2  --config gunicorn_config.py`
* open the / url
* `worker_abort` is not called despite the `WORKER TIMEOUT` event


## Your environment

* ubuntu 14.04
* python 3.6
* gunicorn (19.7.1), aiohttp (3.0.2) (both up-to-date)

Thanks !

issue_title: HTTP pipelining for HTTP-server-side
issue_body:
Does Aiohttp 2.0 support pipelining ? I know, that NGINX HTTP parser supports pipelining. But what about Aiohttp ? Does it handle the case :

1. Request 1 parsed, and is awaiting it's coroutine A to complete
2. Request 2 parsed, and is awaiting it's coroutine B to complete
3. Coroutine B completed. But it's response must not be written to the connection, until A's response is written.

In general, for any TCP-connection (used as HTTP), it is possible to have many coroutines that handle requests simultaneously, and their answers must be strictly ordered. It seems, we should have some queue of responses associated with TCP-connection.

Also, we should be able to specify if pipelining should be used. It have many corner-cases which may be not expected. The same for ClientSession. Should it issue piplined requests or not. In both cases pipelining shuold turned off by default (at least for now).

issue_title: aiohttp.http_exceptions.BadStatusLine caused by extra CRLF after POST data
issue_body:
## Long story short

Some http clients may send extra CRLF after POST data. These extra two bytes are not accounted in Content-Length. It will cause..

```
$ python main.py 
======== Running on http://127.0.0.1:8000 ========
(Press CTRL+C to quit)
Error handling request
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/zarnovic/venv/aiotest/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/web_protocol.py", line 276, in data_received
    messages, upgraded, tail = self._request_parser.feed_data(data)
  File "/home/zarnovic/venv/aiotest/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/http_parser.py", line 115, in feed_data
    msg = self.parse_message(self._lines)
  File "/home/zarnovic/venv/aiotest/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/http_parser.py", line 331, in parse_message
    raise BadStatusLine(line) from None
aiohttp.http_exceptions.BadStatusLine: ''
```

We have noticed this problem after upgrade to `aiohttp` 2.x with `check_http` client (part of Nagios/Icinga).

Bisect shows that this commit https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/commit/51baae652d7ef5f4f8aaefd0ceaa2b28a5f87125 broke it. Seems like all 2.x are affected.

I'm inclined to believe that this is a client's problem. Which, I have reported https://github.com/nagios-plugins/nagios-plugins/issues/266. However, this behavior was "broken" by a change in `aiohttp`, so I'm reporting it also here, for people to find it. Also, it may be a case that `aiohttp` is too strict in parsing and there may be other clients behaving like `check_http`.

Feel free to close it, if you believe it is not `aiohttp`'s problem.

## Expected behaviour

See linked ticket.

## Actual behaviour

See linked ticket.

## Steps to reproduce

See linked ticket.

## Your environment

```
aiohttp (2.0.5)

$ /usr/lib64/nagios/plugins/check_http --version
check_http v2.0.3 (nagios-plugins 2.0.3)
```

There is no proxy/nat/firewall between them. It is reproducible on localhost.

issue_title: Record.annotate() behavior
issue_body:
Is it correct behavior for method `Record.annotate`?

```
def annotate(self, value, ts):
        if value == CLIENT_SEND:
            self.kind(CLIENT)
            self._timestamp = ts
        elif value == SERVER_RECEIVED:
            self.kind(SERVER)
            self._timestamp = ts
        elif value == CLIENT_RECEIVED:
            self.kind(CLIENT)
            self.finish(ts)
        elif value == SERVER_SEND:
            self.kind(SERVER)
            self.finish(ts)
        else:
            v = {'value': value, 'timestamp': int(ts)}
            self._annotations.append(v)
        return self
```

What if user want to send `"cs"` value as annotation, like `span.annotate('cs')`? And user want to see it as annotation, not as timestamp correction.
For example, I log every request body of inbound requests in my API and some one sent `sr` in request body. In this case, I will not see anything in the annotation.
It can be any other example in which we store some random data as an annotation.

If you want to keep the current behavior, you can make the value of `CLIENT_SEND` (and others) as instance of the class inherited from `str`. And in the `annotate` method check for `isinstance`, not for equivalence

This means that "make" aborts with an error message, and pocketsphinx cannot be built successfully. I can confirm that I get the exact same error message that he did.

Any chance this could be fixed soon, so new versions of pocketsphinx will compile again? Thanks for your efforts!

Best,
Alexander.

issue_title: Add override for submitting work as another user (guest accounts).
issue_body:
It would be occasionally convenient to have a flag to override the default submission id for students using guest accounts. I.e., nbgrader submit --id= Can this be implemented without being a security liability? One possibility is to tag the actual submitter's account id (and/or hostname) and flag something in the metadata if it differs from the override. @jhamrick commented:
Hmm, I’m not sure how this would work from a security standpoint. Currently it always submits assignments as the username of the user submitting the assignment, so the instructor can always be sure who submitted what. But if you allow students to specify an id, then what’s to stop one student from submitting a blank assignment using another student’s id?

issue_title: Create a "submit" notebook extension
issue_body: Rather than requiring students to run `nbgrader submit` from the command line (or by creating a new code cell with `!nbgrader submit`), there could be a new button added to the toolbar (or a menu option) that would allow them to submit their assignment. The tricky bit with this, I think, is that I think it would be confusing to have them be able to submit each notebook individually -- it would really be better to have one place to submit the entire assignment. Perhaps something like this could be added to the dashboard?

issue_title: Bug: when editing text, quote should only skip if it's the second of a matched pair.
issue_body:
I like the auto-insert of matching quotes, and I think it's the desired behaviour to have pressing quote skip over the second of a matching pair, but it's not good (also non-standard afaik, at least in emacs it doesn't do this) to have it for the first, it makes it hard to insert new elements in arrays of strings. To illustrate:

Desired:
```
    ['i want the cursor to skip over the quote when i'm done']
                                              cursor ->    ^
```
Undesired:

    ['<-- i am trying to insert a new string in my array to the left here']
    ^ <- cursor

Delete or move the file named `code.py` and the issues magically go away.

issue_title: sovrin_sign and sovrin_verify_signature should support arbitrary byte array
issue_body:
Right now, `sovrin_sign` and `sovrin_verify_signature` can create/verify signatures for the JSON messages that are sent to the ledger. In these methods, a JSON message is serialized to a byte array using a custom algorithm that is not used outside of Indy/Sovrin ledger communication. Also, the signature is automatically inserted into the JSON message.

I think it would be very useful if those methods could be used more generically, to create/verify signatures on any arbitrary byte array supplied by the library user.

issue_title: Progress Bar could use some work.
issue_body:
Right now each file is counted as one thing. So if you just upload one giant file it goes
0% -> 100% and looks like it has hung. A better way would be to count how many chunks are in a file and use that count to have a more accurate progress bar.

issue_title: stop using bare except:
issue_body: A more recent version of flake8 has started failing on bare except, which is very reasonable. https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/tree/bareexcept is a first attempt to get rid of them. That fails a git plugin test, because previously we were using bare except to suppress a `sys.exit` call. (Horrifying!) We need to clean up the shell-out helper a little bit, so that callers that want to handle errors have the option of doing that.

issue_title: Get rid of requests dependency
issue_body:
Per a conversation with Coffee in #matrix-dev:matrix.org, requests is a rather heavyweight dependency when compared to the size of this library. Since we only use requests in the `_send` api method, using urllib instead of requests shouldn't add much extra burden to maintaining the sdk.

The biggest issue to overcome is rewriting all of the tests that use responses.

See also https://github.com/Matrixcoffee/urllib-requests-adapter

issue_title: Update simple_broker
issue_body:
`search` should be called `get_events` and it should take `headers` *or* `start_time`, `end_time` as arguments.

A new function `search_runs` should pass through to MDS `find_header`.

issue_title: replay refactor
issue_body:
Instead of having the `DataMuggler` emit a signal which is connected up to the `models` (as currently implemented), have a (set?) of timers on replay which trigger polling of MDS/databroker.  If there is new data, grab it,  add it to the  muggler and then re-generate the plots.

This has the benefits of simplifying the logic of replay  and means we do not need a Qt aware version of `DataMuggler`.

The cost is that we abandon the idea of having flexible signal/slot based pipelines and make all of the data updating/plotting synchronous.

issue_body:
after pip installing cgcloud-core, cgcloud-toil, cgcloud-mesos and s3am (all with `--pre` because otherwise I got errors), I tried 

`cgcloud --help`
and got
```
ERROR: Cannot find plugin module 'cgcloud.bd2k.ci'. Running 'pip install cgcloud-bd2k-ci' may fix this.
```
However, `pip install  cgcloud-bd2k-ci` (with and without `--pre`) results in 
```
Could not find any downloads that satisfy the requirement cgcloud-bd2k-ci
Cleaning up...
No distributions at all found for cgcloud-bd2k-ci
Storing debug log for failure in /home/ubuntu/.pip/pip.log
```

I then noticed I'd not installed toil itself, so did `pip install toil`
But then `cgcloud --help`
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/data/git/toil_varscan/venv/bin/cgcloud", line 5, in <module>
    from pkg_resources import load_entry_point
  File "/data/git/toil_varscan/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources.py", line 2720, in <module>

```

`pip list`
```

issue_title: Make ComputeResource a base class
issue_body:
A compute resource can have one of several providers. They include:

* Docker
* EC2
* GCE
* Libvirt
* Openstack
* Ovirt
* Rackspace
* Vmware

Each of these types of compute resources is significantly different. For example, here's a snippet from POST /api/compute_resources:

> compute_resource[url]
> required
> URL for Libvirt, RHEV, and Openstack
> Value: String

The `url` field is required, but *only if* the provider is one of libvirt, RHEN or openstack. Similar caveats apply to each of the other fields. As a result, methods such as `create_missing` are a mess. And attributes such as `ComputeResource.url.required` are much less meaningful.

A good way of working around this issue is to create a class hierarchy like this:

    ComputeResource
    DockerComputeResource
    LibvirtComputeResource

Doing this will allow field attributes such as "required" to be more meaningful, and it will help simplify methods such as `create_missing`.

issue_title: Removing agents is causing duplication issue
issue_body:
Hi,

As a disclaimer, I'm new to both Python and Mesa.

I'm working on an ABM of attrition. To debug my model, I've pared the code down to a really simple model. You have N number of agents, at each time step, agents have a certain probability of attriting. If the agent attrits, the agent is removed/killed from the simulation. For each agent, I track a Month variable (a tick in the simulation represents one month). In this very simple version, the Month should match the Step in the model. However, this is not always the case. For some reason, the Month value sometimes repeats. I see this in a csv output of the model results. So we might have an agent with Steps 0, 1, 2, 3, but then the corresponding Month might be 0, 1, 1, 2 (the Month that repeats is often not the month that the agent attrits). I'm using the following line of code to kill the agents when they attrit: model.schedule.remove(self). If I do not "kill" agents, the duplication issue with the Month variable is resolved. However, I will need a way to remove agents for my bigger model.

Any ideas? I'm happy to provide the complete code for my simple model. Thanks.

issue_title: Superseded by official PyQt5 wheels?
issue_body:
Hey! I think this project was very useful back when PyQt5 didn't have official Windows wheels (thank you!), but I wonder if it's still needed? I can see various issues with it:

- People report PyQt/Qt bugs here because it sounds like an official PyQt thing.
- People find it on PyPI instead of the official PyQt5 and install it, and then either get an ancient Qt 5.3, or run into problems because of it not working (in the `#pyqt` IRC channel, we spent around 5h with someone having a "The specified procedure could not be found." error when importing PyQt5.QtCore, and it was really hard to find out that they didn't in fact install the official PyQt5).

So I wonder if there's still a reason for this to exist I'm missing? If not, maybe it should at the very least be deleted from PyPI, as Qt 5.3 is insecure, buggy, and in general not very useful nowadays?

Also, in either case, I think it would make everyone's life easier if this was clearly marked as unofficial and pointing to the official way to install PyQt via pip too.

issue_title: Lazy URL load for OSCAR_HOMEPAGE in defaults.py raises unhandled exception
issue_body:
### Issue Summary

We are seeing the error `'promotions' is not a registered namespace` in our log handling when the Oscar app urls are resolved. It looks like the default settings are lazy loading the promotion homepage for the `OSCAR_HOMEPAGE` setting: https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/defaults.py#L8

Since we're not using the promotions app urls in our customized integration, we see this raise the unhandled exception above.

### Steps to Reproduce

We customize/override the order and payment apps, but otherwise included `oscar.get_core_apps` in our `INSTALLED_APPS` setting. In our urls.py, we did not add `url(r'', include(oscar.app.application.urls))`, and then finally this error is raised when the django app urls are resolved, quickly reproducible in a view that simply raises via `response = handle_uncaught_exception(request, get_resolver(get_urlconf()), sys.exc_info())` in django.core.handlers.exception.py

### Technical details

* Python version: 3.6.3
* Django version: 1.11.7
* Oscar version: 1.6.3

issue_title: Doc improvement proposal - what happens after place an order.
issue_body:
Hello - When implementing the [Place an order](https://django-oscar-api.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage/communicate_with_the_api.html#place-an-order-checkout) api I noticed a confirmation email is never sent. I presume this is because I am expected to create the payment logic myself and only send the email once payment is confirmed. 

For reference Oscar sends the email in a view after running place_order. See [here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/f5d4c174621371e1e4c173e8f850fea6c5d6e9be/src/oscar/apps/checkout/mixins.py#L116). The order api calls place_order but not the handle_successful_order method that involves sending the order.

My proposal is just to note this in the "place an order" section of the docs that it is expected the developer will call send_confirmation_message themselves once the order is final if they want the email sent. Unless there is a better way. I'm happy to send a pull request doc change if this my understanding is correct.

issue_title: brew link --force openssl not working
issue_body:
Hello,
When installing  (.net core)[https://www.microsoft.com/net/core#macos] you have to install the openssl dependency but when trying to link with `brew link --force openssl` is not working, I've got that error:
`Linking /usr/local/Cellar/openssl/1.0.2h_1... 
Error: Could not symlink share/man/man5/config.5ssl
/usr/local/share/man/man5 is not writable.`

I've found a solution just executing that command: 
`sudo chown -R `whoami`:admin /usr/local/share` 
I think it should be pointed out in the official web page. I've tried in different mac laptops with latest OSX(10.11.6) and it's all the time failing so it should be a common problem for everyone.
I don't know if I'm right or not or I'm doing something wrong but I think we should provide better instructions for "non windows users", by the way +1 for the workaround using oh-my-zsh.

Thanks!!

issue_title: Misleading example in closed beta invite email
issue_body:
One of the example the the closed beta invite email suggests using the following:
  ./letsencrypt-auto certonly -a standalone \
  -d example.com -d http://www.example.com \
  --server https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory --agree-dev-preview

This gives an error because it can't verify the domain. It can't verify the domain because the regexp used to verify the domain doesn't consider the 'http://' in 'http://www.example.com' to be valid. The regexp is  in 'letsencrypt/configuration.py'
```
156     # FQDN checks from
157     # http://www.mkyong.com/regular-expressions/domain-name-regular-expression-example/
158     #  Characters used, domain parts < 63 chars, tld > 1 < 7 chars
159     #  first and last char is not "-"
160     fqdn = re.compile("^((?!-)[A-Za-z0-9-]{1,63}(?<!-)\\.)+[A-Za-z]{2,6}$")
161     if any(True for d in domains if not fqdn.match(d)):
162         raise errors.ConfigurationError("Requested domain is not a FQDN")
```
Either the regexp or the example should probably be amended.

issue_title: Don't autodeploy revoked versions

issue_title: Document how to use per-recipient substitution data, metadata, etc.
issue_body:
The [Transmissions API](https://developers.sparkpost.com/api/transmissions.html) supports substitution data, metadata and tags are the recipient level, but there's no explanation of how to do this in the documentation. Only this text in https://python-sparkpost.readthedocs.io/en/latest/resources/transmissions.html :

> Substitution data can be specified at the template, transmission and recipient levels. The order of precedence is as follows: recipient overrides transmission overrides template.

For reference, substitution data is supported by passing in a dictionary to the send method. E.g.:

```python
response = sp.transmissions.send(
...
  recipients = [
    {
      'address': { 'email': 'recipient@somewhere.example' },
      'substitution_data': { 'name': 'Rachael' }
    }
  ],
...
)
```

The same may work for metadata and tags, but I have not tested that (yet).

I'm using version 1.3.5.

issue_title: Unable to retrieve schema by subject if schema id = 0
issue_body:
Description
===========

Unable to retrieve schema by subject if schema id = 0. 

We have a staging and production environment, and in the staging environment the schema in question has id=163 and everything works fine. In production, the id=0 and we can't produce messages.

**Offending line: https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/blob/master/confluent_kafka/avro/serializer/message_serializer.py#L99-L101 -- should probably change `if not schema_id:` to `if schema_id is None:`**

Partial stacktrace:

```
 File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/easyavro/producer.py", line 71, in produce
 value=r[1]
 File "/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/confluent_kafka/avro/__init__.py", line 64, in produce

confluent_kafka.avro.serializer.ValueSerializerError: Unable to retrieve schema id for subject obs-with-qc-value
```

Here's the (truncated) schema, from http://SR_PROD_BASE_URL/subjects/obs-with-qc-value/versions/1

```
{
subject: "obs-with-qc-value",
version: 1,
id: 0,
schema: "{ ... }"
}

How to reproduce
================

1. Have a schema with id: 0  (*)
1. Try to send a message on a topic with that schema, using `Producer.produce`
1. The producer fails to find the schema: `Unable to retrieve schema id for subject`

\* I'm not sure how exactly we ended up with a schema with id 0 -- I guess it was just the first one created after we set up the schema registry.

issue_title: Refactor tzwin as a tzrange subclass
issue_body:
When working on #249, I realized that all the "time zone" parts of `tzwinbase` are essentially just a different implementation of `tzrange`. I think it's probably a good idea to refactor the library such that `tzrange` is the base class for `tzwin`.

This, along with #253, can be part of a general unification of all the interface of the time zone classes.

issue_title: EXIF datetime format is unsupported
issue_body:
Photos with EXIF data contain date and time when the image was taken.
The format is YYYY:MM:DD HH:MM:SS. This results in an incorrectly parsed datetime.

Example:
```python
>>> from dateutil.parser import parse
>>> parse("2015:04:15 17:15:34")
datetime.datetime(2016, 5, 25, 17, 15, 34)

>>> from datetime import date
>>> date.today()
datetime.date(2016, 5, 25)
```

The example shows that the time is parsed correctly, but the date is replaced by the current date.

Tested with dateutil version 2.5.3.

The EXIF specification is over here: <http://www.cipa.jp/std/documents/e/DC-008-2012_E.pdf>
The format is specified on pages (PDF count) 44, 53, 54.

issue_title: InvalidOperation ConversionSyntax after replacing float with Decimal (Refs #570)
issue_body:
Hey there,
Thanks for a great package!

We encountered an issue with 2.7.0 release and exactly this change (float to Decimal):
https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/pull/570/files#diff-4b65e7edb2b73639d2d3360fa8d05343R937

My environment:
```
>>> import dateutil; print(dateutil.__version__)
2.7.0

13:16 $ python --version
Python 3.6.4
```

Small code example:
Before release it was float and producing `ValueError`:
```
>>> float('TEST')
ValueError: could not convert string to float: 'TEST'
```
After release, same operation goes with `Decimal`, which produces a different error:
```
>>> Decimal('TEST')
decimal.InvalidOperation: [<class 'decimal.ConversionSyntax'>]
```

I am not 100% familiar with `dateutil` code, but I see, that `ValueError` was covered in `dateutil.parser._parser.parser#_parse` here: https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/parser/_parser.py#L689-L825

But now that piece is raising `decimal.InvalidOperation: [<class 'decimal.ConversionSyntax'>]` which is not covered at all and breaking the code, that uses it.


What shall we do in that case? Shall we wrap it in `try/except` like it says in leftovers `TODO` comment?

issue_title: Reconnect on lost network interface
issue_body:
When loosing the network connection (e.g. on mobile devices when switching between different wifis or mobile data), using `client.reconnect()` does not work. I think one needs to drop and create a new socket in this case.

One workaround would be to periodically invoke some request (e.g. `GetStateRequest`). In case `TimeoutError` is raised, one can create a completely new instance of `TelegramClient`.

But I guess there already is some kind of ping between server and client, to test whether the connection is still alive? A better solution would be to have some subscribable event or callback which is triggered if this ping signal the connection is lost. Then each application could decide what to, e.g. wait and try reconnect or create a new client instance.

Also, some function like `client.is_connected()` would be nice.

issue_title: Nested Feature Groups
issue_body:
I'm currently investigating whether or not it would be possible to create nested feature groups, for example to place markers on a map divided by year, and then divided again by month.

I've tried experimenting with something like:

``` python

year = folium.FeatureGroup(name='2015')
month = folium.FeatureGroup(name='Jan')
month.add_child(folium.Marker([x, y])
year.add_child(month)
map.add_child(year)
map.add_child(folium.map.LayerControl())
```

But this doesn't seem to work - you only get the year on the layer control, though it does contain the marker associated with the month.

Is there a different way of doing this, or is it just not possible with Feature Groups?

issue_title: Wrong sysfs filenames in battery segment?
issue_body:
Hello everybody!

I was looking at why the battery segment (segments/common/bat.py) wasn't working on my machine. Turned out it was looking for batteries in sysfs and specifically the files `energy_(full|now)`, which don't exist on my machine (Linux 4.5). Seems they are prefixed with `charged` instead `energy`. Changing it resulted in usual energy readings :).

Sven

issue_title: ElasticSearch.create_index sends PUT request with body=None
issue_body:
The `send_request` helper defaults `body` to `''`, but `create_index` defaults `body` to `settings`, which in turn, defaults to `None`, so `send_request` ends up with `body=None`. This causes problems when going down to `httplib` via `urllib3` because `httplib` doesn't set a `Content-Length` header for any requests where the `body` is `None`. All of this amounts to 411 responses from nginx when using that as a proxy for ElasticSearch.

I've actually submitted a bug report for Python because I think that even with `body=None`, `httplib` should set the `Content-Length` header for methods that expect a body (`PATCH`, `POST`, and `PUT`) but in the meantime, we could avoid the issue either by not passing `body` to `send_request` from `create_index` if it's `None`, or (even better) have some logic inside `send_request` that defaults `body` to `None` and sets it to `''` if that's how it comes in.

issue_title: Needs option to not follow links
issue_body:
The 'b2 sync' command follows links and treats them as if they are simply directory paths.

It should either

- not follow links and report them as things it couldn't back up (which is what rclone does)

- follow them but give an option not to follow

Otherwise, a single ln command can radically alter backups.

I'm aware of exclude regex but that is a very crude tool and doesn't provide forward security...e.g., every time you symlink (or software you install unknowingly symlinks), you have to modify the backup script.

issue_title: Suggestion: Add option to end validation after first error
Does not preserve timezone naive format?
issue_body:
Currently, a datetime in timezone naive format (e.g. from datatime.utcnow()) is converted to timezone aware (+00:00) in serialization, but is not converted back to timezone naive in deserialization.  This means that the serialization/deserialization process is not really symmetrical and you can't really use timezone naive formats.  If that was intended, it should be documented.  If this is a bug, I'm happy to look at producing a pull request.

Here is a test demonstrating what I am referring to:

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields, post_load
from datetime import *

class Test(object):
    def __init__(self, ts):
        self.timestamp = ts

class TestSchema(Schema):
    timestamp = fields.DateTime()

    @post_load
    def make_code(self, data):
        instance = Test.__new__(Test)
        instance.__dict__.update(data)
        return instance

now = datetime.utcnow()
test = Test(now)
print test.timestamp
assert(now == test.timestamp)

dump = TestSchema().dump(test).data
load = TestSchema().load(dump).data
print load.timestamp
assert(now == load.timestamp)
```

Output:
```
$ python mmtimetest.py
2015-10-25 23:23:23.489722
2015-10-25 23:23:23.489722+00:00
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "mmtimetest.py", line 25, in <module>
    assert(now == load.timestamp)
TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes
```

issue_title: Different date deserialisation behaviour when dateutil present
issue_body:
We ran into an issue with date deserialisation - which is different depending on the presence of `python-dateutil`.

Our API does not accept UK/US style date formats (eg. `13/12/2016` or `12/13/2016`). The `fields.Date`  [type](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/fields.py#L934]) says it only supports ISO8601 format, yet when `python-dateutil` is present it actually accepts ISO and the examples above.

I don't think this changing behaviour at runtime is A Good Thing. I suppose we could write our own strict ISO8601 validator?

Making `python-dateutil` required as in #349 would fix this - except for the misleading comments in `fields.Date`.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Activity Classifier
issue_body:
I was trying to reproduce the [Activity Classifier](https://github.com/apple/turicreate/blob/master/userguide/activity_classifier/introduction.md) example by following along and quickly got stuck as the example appears to skip over the essential part of generating the contents of `hapt_data.sframe` and how to actually run the example snippet.

Apart from the missing yet crucial step from `HAPT Data Set.zip` to `hapt_data.sframe` it is also not clear to somebody new to turicreate how to invoke the example snippet.

The example falls short on a couple of questions:

- How does one generate or get ahold of the file `hapt_data.sframe`?
- Where does one have to place the `hapt_data.sframe` file?
- Where does one have to place the example's snippet? Within `~/venv/`, or outside?
- How does one actually run the example? Via `python ./activity_classifier.py`?
- Does one first have to `cd` into `~/venv/` before running the example file via `python …`?
- Where does one find the produced output (`mymodel.model` and `MyActivityClassifier.mlmodel`)?

It is very nice to have such a wide range of applications and examples for most (if not all) of them for turicreate.

But an example that cannot be followed through without prior knowledge or filling in the blanks à la `here be dragons` at the end of the day is of little to no use for anybody new to the project/codebase. 😟

issue_title: Example for how to use existing config functions
issue_body:
Related to #193 but more pragmatic. 

I typically structure my projects like this:

```
def generate_foo_config(*args, **kwargs): 
       # just manually defining foo_config['arg'] = arg
        foo_config = {....}
        return foo_config

def pipeline_foo(foo_config):
      # parse foo_config and do foo task
``` 

Where I have a pair of those methods for foo = data, train, model.  Then the main method for the whole experiment looks like this:

```
def pipeline_run(data_config, train_config, model_config):
      full_config = {
           'data': data_config,
           'train': train_config,
           'model': model_config
     }
     # load pipelined batch gen, model and run pipelined training
```

What would the best way to go about integrating this scheme with `sacred`?  There isn't a way to directly add the configs using `generate_foo_config`, right? I have to call all `generate_foo_config` with arbitrary args to get the structure of the config dicts, compose them into `full_config`, and then pass that to `experiment.add_config`? 

So something like this:

```
from experiment_pkg.train import generate_train_config, pipeline_train
from experiment_pkg.model import generate_model_config, pipeline_model
from experiment_pkg.data import generate_data_config, pipeline_batch_gen

from sacred import Experiment

experiment = Experiment('foo_bar')

full_config = {
    'data': generate_data_config(....),
    'train': generate_train_config(....),
    'model': generate_model_config(...)
}


```

issue_title: Removing Z3_get_error_msg_ex breaks backwards compatibility
issue_body:
I develop unofficial Haskell bindings for Z3's API and one my users have noticed that you have removed ```Z3_get_error_msg_ex```. This change was made by commit bea68cd194b0 ("remove deprecated API functionality").

I know that ```Z3_get_error_msg``` has been deprecated for some time, and consequently I replaced it with ```Z3_get_error_msg_ex```. Removing ```Z3_get_error_msg``` it's fine, or even making ir a synonym of ```Z3_get_error_msg_ex```. But removing ```Z3_get_error_msg_ex``` breaks backwards compatibility of Z3 4.4.2 with respect to 4.4.1, which is not nice. One would like to assume that any 4.4.y version will be backwards compatible with 4.4.x for y > x.

Could you instead deprecate ```Z3_get_error_msg_ex``` and remove it later in Z3 4.5 or later?

Thanks,

issue_title: alternative pattern annotations for forall quantifiers are sometimes ignored by Z3
issue_body:
When a forall quantifier is provided with multiple alternative patterns, it seems that Z3 sometimes doesn't use anything but the first pattern. The behaviour seems quite sensitive to the kind of example, but I've managed to cut down one to a fairly reasonable Boogie input (and corresponding smt file). 

Here is the Boogie input:
[Z3_repro.bpl.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/35442/Z3_repro.bpl.txt)

The axiom starting on line 31 exhibits the problematic behaviour. Uncommenting the alternative version of this axiom below it (or just removing its first trigger annotation in this particular example) will make the file verify. Similarly, one can remove the first pattern annotation from the corresponding quantifier in the smt file, at line 184:
[triggering_repro.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/35441/triggering_repro.smt2.txt)

This could be related to the following (old) stack overflow question, where Leonardo confirmed and fixed a bug with [very similar symptoms.](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/11649305/triggering-problems-in-z3)

issue_title: A problem when get model for a SMT-OPT problem by Z3
issue_body:
I encode a ite-branch as following:
"
(declare-fun flag () Bool)
(declare-fun x () Int)
(declare-fun y () Int)
(declare-fun z () Int)
(assert 
  (let ((a!1 (and (> 50 z)
                  (= y (+ x 1))
                  (= flag true)))
       )
  (let ((a!3 (and (>= z 50)
                  (= y (- x 1))
                  (= flag false)))
       )
  (and true
       (or a!1 a!3)))))
**(assert (>= x 0))**
(maximize y)
(check-sat)
(get-model)
"
I get the model like this :
"
y |-> oo
sat
(model 
  **(define-fun flag () Bool
    false)**
  (define-fun z () Int
    50)
  (define-fun y () Int
    (- 1))
  (define-fun x () Int
    0)
)
"
**Why the interpretation for declare-fun "flag" is "false"?**
I think it should be "true", because "true" corresponds the branch "y = x + 1", while "false" is for the branch "y = x - 1".
When I delete the assertion "**(assert (>= x 0))**" in the constraint, I get the model like this :
"
y |-> oo
sat
(model 
  (define-fun flag () Bool
    true)
  (define-fun z () Int
    0)
  (define-fun y () Int
    0)
  (define-fun x () Int
    (- 1))
)
"
The interpretation for flag is "true" now (This model fit my expection). But I think this assertion "(assert (>= x 0))" is independent for the model. Is this a bug?

Our options are:

2. Use travis-ci on this repo to build and push a docker image. This requires setting `sudo: true` in travis.yml, which is going to slow down your tests (there's no way to do that only for post-merge builds I can tell). Not sure if you want this

  "repository": "https://github.com/nteract/nteract/tree/master/packages/transform-vdom"
```

You can tell which are missing it either by looking at the `package.json`s directly or even by looking at [the network dependencies](https://github.com/nteract/nteract/network/dependencies) -- they'll show up as the github ghost.

issue_title: support for ELF relocatable objects (i.e. Linux kernel modules)
issue_body:
As you can see in https://github.com/angr/cle/blob/82e33f92e18d7b5a8cfb9ed43663d50a52a4ab43/cle/backends/elf.py#L491, the ELF backend uses sh_addr for loading objects into the memory. 
However, for relocatable object the `sh_addr` is always zero (only `sh_offset` is set) which makes the binary loading fail.

issue_title: lift of self modyfing code may fail due to max_size being to big
issue_body:
When lifting a block of code in [vex/engine.vex](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/engines/vex/engine.py#L117) through CFGAccurate for instance, the size of the block is left to default which is then set to [VEX_IRSB_MAX_SIZE](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/engines/vex/engine.py#L406) (400 bytes).

When considering self modyfing code, the memory is loaded using state [through](https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/engines/vex/engine.py#L529) 
```python
buff = state.se.eval(state.memory.load(addr, max_size, inspect=False), cast_to=str)
```

Since max_size is set to 400, the evaluation is done on the 400 bytes. The evaluation can then fail if there are unsolvable constraints after the block we want to lift.

issue_title: Question: how to pass namespace defintion to xpath filter?
issue_body:
Hi,

I would like to pass an xpath filter to the `get` method. It should be the equivalent of this XML request:

```xml
<rpc xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0" message-id="28">
  <get>
    <filter type="subtree">
      <turing-machine xmlns="http://example.net/turing-machine">
        <transition-function />
      </turing-machine>
    </filter>
  </get>
</rpc>
```

I wanted to use something like:
```python
reply = connection.get(filter=("xpath", "/tm:turing-machine/tm:transition-function"))
```

This produces an invalid request, since `tm` namespace is not defined anywhere:
```xml
<nc:rpc xmlns:nc="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0" message-id="urn:uuid:6f210162-f037-47ee-93cd-b3034b0100ac">
  <nc:get>
    <nc:filter type="xpath" select="/tm:turing-machine/tm:transition-function"/>
  </nc:get>
</nc:rpc>
```

(Netopeer2 server returns an error: `XML namespace with prefix "tm" not defined.`)

Correct request would look like (this is how `netopeer2-cli` produces it):
```xml
<rpc xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0" message-id="30">
  <get>
    <filter type="xpath" xmlns:tm="http://example.net/turing-machine" select="/tm:turing-machine/transition-function"/>
  </get>
</rpc>
```

How this could be achieved with `ncclient`?

issue_title: Enhanced feature: Allow to derive the band integrated radiance
issue_body:
#### Code Sample, a minimal, complete, and verifiable piece of code

```
from pyspectral.radiance_tb_conversion import RadTbConverter
from pyspectral.utils import debug_on
debug_on()
modis = RadTbConverter('EOS-Aqua', 'modis', '20')
res = modis.tb2radiance(281., '20', lut=False)

```
#### Problem description

When the above code is run, the spectral radiance for the MODIS band 20 is derived.
One issue is that it should actually not be needed to pass the band to the method. It should
actually not be allowed to derive the radiance for another band.

But, my main issue, is that it should also be possible to derive the band integrated radiance.
This can be derived by not dividing with the integral of the relative spectral response for the given 
band. So something like this:
```
modis = RadTbConverter('EOS-Aqua', 'modis', '20')
res = modis.tb2radiance(281., lut=False, normalized=False)

Thank you for reporting an issue !

issue_title: Problem with configuration system
issue_body:
A bug has been introduced in the configuration system which causes out-of-date values to be locked into a users `.gangarc` file. Two instances of this are
   * Starting Ganga as ganga -o'[Logging]Ganga.Lib=DEBUG' will if it at the same time is a release that the user has not used before
   * Values that are changed in `.INI` file are likewise locked into the users .gangarc when the `,gangarc` file is recreated with a new release.

As an example, my `.gangarc` file at Imperial has the values below uncommented

```
load_default_Dirac_backend = False
splitFilesChunks = 5000

issue_body:
just want to test how much pytraj is slower than cpptraj.

info
```python
In [18]: traj
Out[18]:
<pytraj.TrajectoryIterator with 1000 frames: 

<Topology with 17443 atoms, 5666 residues, 5634 mols, 17452 bonds, 
PBC with box type = truncoct>>
```

cpptraj speed:
```python
In [16]: %%time
pt.datafiles.cpptraj_dry_run("""
parm ./myparm.top
trajin remd.x.000
autoimage
rms first @CA
matrix dist @CA""")
   ....:
CPU times: user 3.5 s, sys: 346 ms, total: 3.85 s
Wall time: 3.85 s
```

pytraj speed
```python
In [17]: %time pt.matrix_analysis.distance_matrix(traj(autoimage=True, rmsfit=(0, '@CA')), 
                                                                                  '@CA')
CPU times: user 4.12 s, sys: 358 ms, total: 4.48 s
Wall time: 4.48 s
```

overall, it tooks about **3.5 s** for cpptraj while **4.12 s** for pytraj.

Now I only need to worry about memory leak in some parts of pytraj.

issue_title: [question] Why does SplashTextResponse inherits from TextResponse instead HtmlResponse?
issue_body:
Why does `scrapy_splash.reponse.SplashTextResponse` inherits from `scrapy.http.reponse.text.TextResponse` and not from `scrapy.http.reponse.html.HtmlResponse`?

I understand that they are identical, and `HtmlResponse` uses `TextResponse` without any change, but they mean different things and Scrapy uses `isinstance` check to distinguish them.

In my case, it causes problem because I have type check for `HtmlResponse` where I expect to get HTML page and use `css` method to extract data from it, but `SplashTextResponse` doesn't pass it.

issue_title: Show more information on assert_response when status code fails
issue_body:
Right now `assert_response` first checks the `status_code`:
https://github.com/4Catalyzer/flask-resty/blob/289e4fff0909b54c8677701f87a4ee393099efc7/flask_resty/testing.py#L106
however this doesn't print any information as to what the error was other than the status code. In many cases, seeing the contents of errors is helpful for diagnosing the problem. This is especially true when a 200 is expected but some other error happens yielding a 4XX error. 

I suggest something like:
```python
def assert_response(response, expected_status_code, expected_data=UNDEFINED):
    if 200 <= response.status_code < 300:
        response_data = get_data(response)
    else:
        response_data = get_errors(response)

    assert response.status_code == expected_status_code, response_data

    if expected_data is UNDEFINED:
        return

...
```

issue_title: SR300 multible camera couses freezes in uvc-wmf.h
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | SR300 | 
| Firmware Version |  3.15.0.0 | 
| Operating System & Version |  Windows 10   |
| Build System |  VS2014 | 

Currently I'm experimenting with 5 RS300 Cameras, each of them connected to it's own usb controler.
It's working fine for most situations, but does couse a program freeze at some points ( sometimes, but the more cameras are connected, the more often they occure )

Setting parameters via

`void set_control(device & device, const extension_unit & xu, uint8_t ctrl, void *data, int len)` (uvc-wmf.h)

can freeze the whole program without a chance of recovering.

This can be reproduced by attaching more than one camera and simple keep activating and deactivating the laser of one of the camera.

Once one of the RS300 did crash the context is no longer able to be created ( even after restarting the program)
It will freeze at 

`WinUsb_WritePipe(usb_interface_handle, endpoint, (PUCHAR)buffer, bufferLength, &lengthWritten, NULL); ` (uvc-wmf.cpp)

sometimes during the **usb_synchronous_write** but also at **usb_synchronous_read** .

These problems do not occure using the F200 cameras ( tried it with 7 F200 cameras without any problems)

issue_title: Considering refactoring “analysis” tasks as builders
DomainIntentDeterminationEngine
issue_body: An alternate implementation (or perhaps just an extension of IntentDeterminationEngine) would allow for specifying a "domain" (reminders, music, etc) when registering vocabulary and intents. The parser would then create candidate parse results for each domain, and yield in order of confidence (like the current implementation). This will help alleviate some issues where entity matches are breaking up wildcard regex entities (mentioning a radio inside a sms message could break the message body).

issue_title: Stabilize Cert tests behavior
Consider removing otPlatRadioIdle() and associated logic.
issue_body: Currently OT uses otPlatRadioIdle() to transition the radio between receive and transmit.  It might be more natural and straightforward if OT were to instead remove the option for a radio Idle State and instead treat the radio as operating in one of the following States {Disabled, Sleep, Receive, Transmit}.  To transmit a packet OT would then make a call to otPlatRadioTransmit() without a pre-requisite call to otPlatRadioIdle().  This also creates an assumption that if the radio is not Disabled, Transmitting or Sleeping then it must be Receiving.  This should reduce opportunities for missing received packets and simplify the OT code.

issue_title: Implementar función `make_catalogs_backup()`
issue_body:
**Contexto**

Se está necesitando implementar la función `make_catalogs_backup()` para generar una copia de los datos y/o metadatos de uno o más catálogos en data.json, en un filesystem local.

La idea es que mediante una simple función, el usuario de pydatajson pueda generar una copia completa de un catálogo en data.json, descargándose todas las distribuciones y generando una copia de un catálogo en XLSX y JSON generando automáticamente un árbol de paths como el siguiente:

```
./catalog/{catalog_identifier}/catalog.xlsx
./catalog/{catalog_identifier}/data.json
./catalog/{catalog_identifier}/dataset/{dataset_identifier}/distribution/{distribution_identifier}/download/distribution-fileName.csv
```

**Propuesta**

Implementar un nuevo módulo `backup.py` para implementar estas funciones de backup siguiendo el patrón usual.

Implementar una función con la siguiente signature en ese módulo, e incorporarla al objeto `DataJson`:

```
make_catalogs_backup(catalogs=None,
                             local_catalogs_dir="my/local/path/",
                             copy_metadata=True, copy_data=True):
        """Realiza copia de los datos y metadatos de uno o más catálogos.

        Args:
            catalogs (list or dict): Lista de catálogos (elementos que pueden
                ser interpretados por DataJson como catálogos) o diccionario
                donde las keys se interpretan como los catalog_identifier:
                    {
                        "modernizacion": "http://infra.datos.gob.ar/catalog/modernizacion/data.json"
                    }
                Cuando es una lista, los ids se toman de catalog_identifer, y
                se ignoran los catálogos que no tengan catalog_identifier.
                Cuando se pasa un diccionario, los keys reemplazan a los
                catalog_identifier (estos no se leeen).
            local_catalogs_dir (str): Directorio local en el cual se va a crear
                la carpeta "catalog/..." con todos los catálogos.
            copy_metadata (bool): Si es verdadero, se generan los archivos
                data.json y catalog.xlsx.
            copy_data (bool): Si es verdadero, se descargan todas las
                distribuciones de todos los catálogos.

        Return:
            None
        """
```

La función permite crear una copia de datos, metadatos o ambos. Se puede usar en un sólo catálogo o en un conjunto. Siempre crea la carpeta `catalog` para iniciar el árbol de carpetas. No levanta errores si alguna carpeta no existe: crea las que necesita si no existen, o utiliza las que ya existen. Pisa archivos existentes pero no borra los anteriores. Si no se pasa un `local_catalogs_dir` se crea el _working directory_ actual (donde esté la terminal).

lets say i have an autoencoder network

```

encoder = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, samples])
encoder2 = tflearn.fully_connected(encoder, 30)
decoder = tflearn.fully_connected(encoder2, samples, activation='tanh')

In other words turn it into this network:

```
encoder2 = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, 30])
decoder = tflearn.fully_connected(encoder2, samples)
```

issue_title: Too much data transfer when logging to lots of environments
issue_body:
I'm often running experiments where ~20 environments are actively updated, with ~6 plots in each. My logging frequency is very high --- about 5 times a second for each environment.

Previously, visdom's client side would only retrieve data whenever it needed to render --- i.e., when selecting an environment from the dropdown, only its data would be downloaded.

I think that at some point in the past ~3 months (I could be wrong), visdom underwent a frontend update such that data from all environments is downloaded client-side all the time.
This update makes it impossible to use the web UI, as everything is frozen due to high CPU usage (presumably from downloading and de-serializing the many streams of data being logged).
